title: Arbitrary external call via user-controlled swap data enables theft of withdrawn collateral
claim: The `deloopAllCollaterals` function makes an arbitrary low-level call `paraSwapContract.call(paraswapCallData)` at line 340 where both the target contract address (`paraSwapContract`) and the calldata (`paraswapCallData`) are fully user-controlled via the swap data decoded from attacker-supplied bytes. This is reachable through the externally accessible `flashloanReq` function (line 111) which has no access control.
impact: An attacker can craft swap data that makes the Connector call an attacker-controlled contract with arbitrary calldata. In the deleveraging flow (`_opt=true`), the DSA's collateral is withdrawn to the Connector before this call executes, allowing the attacker to steal the collateral by having their swap data redirect tokens to themselves. Only enough tokens need to be left to repay the flashloan for the transaction to succeed, with the rest exfiltrated to the attacker.
paths: Path 1 (via delegateDoughCall - requires DSA with health factor below minDeleveragingRatio and registered connector): Attacker calls DSA.doughCall(22, ...) which delegatecalls to Connector.delegateDoughCall with _opt=true and malicious _swapData â†’ deloopDebtPositions â†’ calculateCollateralAndDebtBeforeFlashLoanData repays debt with aTokens and computes remaining collateral â†’ flashloanReq initiates flashloan â†’ executeOperation callback: repayAllDebtAssetsWithFlashLoan repays DSA debt â†’ extractAllCollaterals withdraws DSA collateral to Connector â†’ deloopAllCollaterals executes attacker's malicious swap data which calls an attacker-controlled contract that transfers most collateral to attacker, leaving only enough to repay flashloan â†’ repayFlashloansAndTransferToTreasury repays flashloan â†’ transaction succeeds, attacker profits by stolen collateral minus flashloan premium

Path 2 (direct flashloanReq - always available): Attacker calls flashloanReq directly with attacker as msg.sender, providing their own collateral tokens and crafting swap data that calls an arbitrary contract with arbitrary calldata â†’ during executeOperation, attacker's contract receives allowance for flashloaned tokens via repayAllDebtAssetsWithFlashLoan, and the arbitrary call in deloopAllCollaterals can perform any action the attacker desires (token approvals, transfers, calls to other protocols)

title: Missing access control on flashloanReq allows anyone to initiate flashloans with arbitrary parameters
claim: `flashloanReq` (line 111) is declared `external` with no access control modifiers and no validation of the caller. The function encodes `msg.sender` as the `dsaAddress` and initiates an Aave V3 flashloan. In the callback `executeOperation` (line 257), `repayAllDebtAssetsWithFlashLoan` (line 347) calls `IERC20(assets[i]).safeIncreaseAllowance(dsaAddress, amounts[i])` and `IDoughDsa(dsaAddress).executeAction(...)`, giving the caller both an allowance for the flashloaned tokens and an arbitrary execution opportunity.
impact: Any address can use this contract as a permissionless Aave V3 flashloan facilitator, completely bypassing the health-factor-based gating in `delegateDoughCall`. A malicious caller can receive approval for flashloaned tokens during the callback and, through their `executeAction` implementation, steal the flashloaned assets. This is a necessary precondition for exploiting F-001 and fundamentally undermines the intended access control model of the protocol.
paths: Attacker calls Connector.flashloanReq(_opt, debtTokens, debtAmounts, debtRateMode, collateralTokens, collateralAmounts, swapData) â†’ Aave Pool flashloans assets to Connector â†’ Pool calls executeOperation â†’ repayAllDebtAssetsWithFlashLoan approves attacker (msg.sender) for flashloaned tokens and calls attacker.executeAction â†’ attacker's executeAction can transferFrom the approved tokens to steal them â†’ attacker must provide equivalent collateral via extractAllCollaterals or the tx reverts

Combined with F-001: Attacker uses swapData to make the Connector call an arbitrary contract that can perform any on-chain action the Connector is authorized for (token approvals, protocol interactions)

title: User-controlled swap data not validated against automatically-determined collateral in opt=true deleveraging path
claim: When `_opt=true`, the function `calculateCollateralAndDebtBeforeFlashLoanData()` (line 116) automatically computes which collateral tokens and amounts to withdraw based on the DSA's actual Aave position. However, the `_swapData` parameter (user-supplied) is passed unchanged to `flashloanReq` (line 104). In the callback, `deloopInOneOrMultipleTransactions` (line 269) uses the automatically-computed `collateralTokens`/`collateralAmounts` for withdrawal but uses the user-supplied `multiTokenSwapData` for the swap. There is no validation that the swap data's `srcToken`/`srcAmount` match the actual collateral being withdrawn.
impact: Even when the system correctly determines what collateral to withdraw, the attacker controls how that collateral is swapped. The swap data can specify a different source token than the withdrawn collateral, causing the `safeIncreaseAllowance` at line 339 to approve a token the Connector doesn't hold while the arbitrary call at line 340 can interact with the tokens the Connector actually holds (the withdrawn collateral). This mismatch enables the attacker to exfiltrate collateral through crafted swap calldata while the approval on an unrelated token acts as a smokescreen.
paths: Attacker triggers deleveraging with _opt=true and provides _swapData where srcToken is a token the Connector has 0 balance of, but the paraswapCallData contains instructions to transfer the actually-withdrawn collateral token to the attacker â†’ safeIncreaseAllowance approves tokenTransferProxy for 0-balance token (harmless) â†’ paraSwapContract.call executes attacker's calldata that does IERC20(actualCollateralToken).transferFrom(connector, attacker, amount) â†’ collateral stolen
